agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform wants to display real-time earnings breakdowns (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) to drivers across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks governing driver classification, compensation transparency, and labor relations.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation / Earnings Transparency","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Base Pay","definition":"The fixed or hourly compensation component paid to a driver for availability or per-shift, distinct from performance-based bonuses. Semantics differ: in California this is an employee wage subject to minimum wage law; in Indonesia it may be a contractor service fee."},{"term":"Per-Delivery Bonus","definition":"Variable compensation awarded for each completed delivery, calculated on top of base pay."},{"term":"Tip","definition":"Gratuity paid by the end customer and passed through to the driver. Must never be counted toward base pay or minimum wage calculations in employee jurisdictions."},{"term":"Deduction","definition":"Any amount subtracted from gross earnings — e.g., equipment fees, insurance contributions, tax withholdings, platform fees. The nature and legality of deductions varies by driver classification and jurisdiction."},{"term":"Driver Classification","definition":"The legal status of the driver — employee (California/AB5), or independent contractor (Indonesia). Determines which labor laws, tax rules, and transparency obligations apply."},{"term":"Works Council (Betriebsrat)","definition":"A legally mandated employee representative body in Germany that must be consulted and must approve changes to systems that affect compensation display or monitoring before rollout."},{"term":"Real-Time Earnings","definition":"Earnings data updated with minimal delay as deliveries are completed. The exact latency tolerance and update trigger are unspecified."},{"term":"Earnings Breakdown","definition":"An itemized view of all compensation components and deductions for a given period, presented to the driver."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Tips must never be counted toward or offset against base pay or minimum wage obligations in employee jurisdictions (California).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating this is a wage theft violation under California Labor Code. This is a legal constraint that cannot be overridden by business policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a California driver, WHEN earnings are calculated, THEN tip amounts are excluded from base pay and minimum wage compliance calculations."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"In California, the earnings breakdown must be displayed to all drivers — this is a legal obligation under wage transparency requirements, not an optional feature.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"AB5 classification as employees triggers mandatory wage statement requirements under California Labor Code §226. Non-compliance creates legal liability.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver operating in California, WHEN they access their earnings, THEN a full itemized breakdown (base pay, bonuses, tips, deductions) is always visible — it cannot be hidden or toggled off."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"In Germany, the earnings transparency feature must NOT be rolled out to drivers until works council (Betriebsrat) approval has been obtained and recorded.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"German Works Constitution Act (BetrVG) §87 gives the works council co-determination rights over monitoring and compensation display systems. Deploying without approval exposes the company to injunctions and fines.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN the Germany locale, WHEN the feature deployment flag is evaluated, THEN it returns disabled unless a works council approval record exists with status 'approved'."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Deductions must be individually itemized — never shown as a single lump sum — so drivers can understand each charge.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a transparency best practice and likely a legal requirement in California (itemized wage statements), but the level of granularity is a policy decision in contractor jurisdictions.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN any driver viewing their earnings breakdown, WHEN deductions exist, THEN each deduction is displayed as a separate line item with a label and amount."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Earnings breakdown components must reflect the driver's legal classification: employee-specific items (tax withholdings, employer contributions) for employees; contractor-specific items (platform fees, self-employment context) for independent contractors.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing employee-style deductions to contractors (or vice versa) misrepresents the legal relationship and creates classification risk — e.g., showing 'tax withholding' to an Indonesian contractor implies employment.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver in Indonesia (contractor), WHEN earnings are displayed, THEN no employee-specific line items (e.g., income tax withholding, benefits contributions) appear in the breakdown."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Earnings data must be computed and displayed in the local currency of the driver's operating jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Displaying earnings in a foreign currency would be misleading and non-compliant with local wage statement laws.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver in Germany, WHEN earnings are displayed, THEN all amounts are denominated in EUR."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A California-based driver has completed 3 deliveries in a shift","when":"The driver views their earnings breakdown","then":"They see itemized base pay (meeting or exceeding minimum wage), 3 per-delivery bonuses, any tips received, and each deduction listed separately with labels — all in USD"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Germany-based driver attempts to access the earnings transparency feature","when":"No works council approval record exists in the system","then":"The feature is unavailable and the driver sees a locale-appropriate message explaining it is not yet active"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Germany-based driver attempts to access the earnings transparency feature","when":"A works council approval record with status 'approved' exists","then":"The driver sees their full earnings breakdown in EUR"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An Indonesia-based driver has completed deliveries","when":"They view their earnings breakdown","then":"They see contractor-appropriate line items (service fees, per-delivery payments, tips, platform fees) with no employee-style withholdings — all in IDR"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A California driver receives a $10 tip on a delivery","when":"The system calculates compliance with minimum wage","then":"The $10 tip is excluded entirely from the minimum wage calculation"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A driver completes a delivery","when":"The earnings breakdown is refreshed","then":"The new delivery's earnings components appear in the breakdown within the defined real-time latency threshold"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A driver in any jurisdiction has earnings with multiple deductions","when":"They view the deductions section","then":"Each deduction is a separate line item with a human-readable label and amount — no aggregate 'total deductions' without itemization"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A driver operates across jurisdiction boundaries (e.g., starts in one state, delivers in California)","expected_behavior":"System must determine which jurisdiction's rules apply to each delivery or shift. This is unspecified and needs clarification — likely the delivery destination or driver's home jurisdiction governs."},{"scenario":"Works council approval in Germany is revoked after initial rollout","expected_behavior":"Feature must be immediately disabled for all German drivers. The system needs a mechanism to revoke access, not just grant it."},{"scenario":"A California driver has deductions that would reduce effective pay below minimum wage","expected_behavior":"System must flag or prevent this — deductions cannot reduce compensation below the legally mandated minimum wage floor."},{"scenario":"A delivery is disputed or reversed after appearing in the real-time breakdown","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown must update to reflect the reversal with a clear line item explaining the adjustment — earnings should never silently decrease."},{"scenario":"Zero-tip display in a tipping culture (California)","expected_behavior":"Show $0.00 tips explicitly rather than hiding the row, so drivers trust the system is accurately reporting all components."},{"scenario":"Indonesia driver sees the feature and infers employee status","expected_behavior":"UI language must be carefully framed for contractors — avoid terms like 'wages', 'salary', 'paycheck' that imply employment. Use 'earnings', 'payment', 'service fees'."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Non-compliance with California wage transparency laws risks class-action lawsuits and regulatory fines. Germany deployment without works council approval risks injunctions. Proper implementation builds driver trust, potentially reducing driver churn (a major cost in logistics).","operational":"Three jurisdiction-specific display variants increase complexity. Works council gating in Germany creates a hard external dependency on rollout timeline. Real-time computation adds infrastructure load per completed delivery.","user":"Drivers gain clarity on how they are paid, increasing trust and satisfaction. However, poorly labeled deductions or confusing UI could backfire — transparency without clarity breeds suspicion.","risk":"HIGH legal risk if California wage statement requirements are not met (Labor Code §226 penalties up to $4,000/employee). MEDIUM risk in Germany if works council process is bypassed. Classification risk if Indonesian contractor UI uses employment language. LOW regulatory risk in Indonesia but reputational risk if breakdown reveals unfavorable fee structures.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers (California)","impact":"Legally entitled to this information — feature is a compliance obligation, not a perk. Empowers them to verify wage law compliance."},{"group":"Drivers (Germany)","impact":"Cannot access feature until works council approves. Potential frustration if other regions get it first."},{"group":"Drivers (Indonesia)","impact":"Receive transparency they are not legally owed — a competitive advantage for driver recruitment, but deduction visibility may cause dissatisfaction."},{"group":"Works Council (Germany)","impact":"Must be engaged as a formal stakeholder with approval authority. Failure to involve them is a legal violation."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance Team","impact":"Must validate jurisdiction-specific display rules, deduction categories, and classification-appropriate language before launch."},{"group":"Finance/Payroll","impact":"Must provide accurate, real-time earnings data feeds with jurisdiction-aware deduction categorization."},{"group":"Product/Engineering","impact":"Must build a jurisdiction-aware earnings display system with feature-flagging, locale-specific business logic, and works council gating."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The feature request is stated as a single global requirement but spans three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks. Several critical design decisions are unspecified: what 'real time' means precisely, what specific deductions exist per jurisdiction, how cross-border deliveries are handled, and whether the works council process has begun. The spec is clear on WHAT to show but ambiguous on HOW jurisdiction-specific rules shape the implementation.","high_confidence_areas":["Tips must not offset base pay/minimum wage in California","Works council approval is a hard prerequisite in Germany","Driver classification must dictate earnings line-item vocabulary","Earnings must be in local currency","California display is legally mandatory, not optional"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact deduction categories per jurisdiction — never enumerated by anyone","Definition of 'real time' — sub-second? per-delivery? end-of-shift?","Cross-jurisdiction delivery handling","Whether works council engagement has started or is purely hypothetical","Tax withholding display requirements per jurisdiction","Whether Indonesia drivers currently see any earnings info that this replaces"],"assumptions":["Each driver is assigned to exactly one primary jurisdiction","The platform already has driver classification data per jurisdiction","Works council approval is a binary gate — not a partial or conditional approval","The feature is additive — no existing earnings display is being removed or replaced"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined. Does this mean sub-second updates as each delivery completes, updates on a polling interval (e.g., every 60 seconds), or on-demand refresh? The infrastructure cost and UX differ dramatically between these interpretations.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Drivers","Finance/Payroll"],"options":["Event-driven: update earnings breakdown immediately when a delivery is marked complete","Polling: refresh every N seconds/minutes","On-demand: update only when the driver opens or refreshes the earnings screen"],"recommendation":"Event-driven update triggered on delivery completion is the most natural fit for 'real time' in a delivery context and avoids unnecessary polling cost. Recommend defining SLA as 'within 30 seconds of delivery completion'."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'deductions' but never enumerates what deductions exist in each jurisdiction. For California employees this could include tax withholdings, benefits, equipment fees. For Indonesian contractors this could include platform commission, insurance opt-in, cash handling fees. The earnings breakdown cannot be built without a defined deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Finance/Payroll","Drivers"],"options":["Define a global superset of deduction categories and show only applicable ones per jurisdiction","Let each jurisdiction define its own deduction taxonomy independently","Start with a minimal set (platform fees, tax withholdings where applicable) and expand iteratively"],"recommendation":"Each jurisdiction should define its own deduction taxonomy reviewed by local legal counsel. A shared interface/schema is fine, but the categories themselves must be jurisdiction-specific to avoid classification risk."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not address whether the earnings breakdown is per-delivery, per-shift, per-day, or per-pay-period. California wage statement law (LC §226) requires specific pay period itemization. Contractor jurisdictions may prefer per-delivery granularity. The time granularity of the breakdown is unspecified.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Legal/Compliance","Product"],"options":["Per-delivery breakdown with rollup to pay-period for California compliance","Per-shift breakdown as the default view","Per-pay-period only, matching traditional wage statements","Drill-down hierarchy: pay period → shift → delivery"],"recommendation":"Implement a drill-down model: default view is the current pay period (required for California compliance), with ability to expand into per-delivery detail. This satisfies legal requirements while giving drivers the granularity they want."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this domain scenario carefully.

This is about a "driver earnings transparency" feature for a global logistics platform operating across three jurisdictions with different legal requirements.

Key observations:
1. California - legally required wage transparency, drivers are employees (AB5)
2. Germany - works council approval needed before rollout
3. Indonesia - independent contractors, no legal requirement

The main issue here: there's missing context. Several critical pieces of information were never provided:

Wait, let me reconsider. Is this ambiguity, missing_context, or contradiction?

- There's no contradiction - the requirements don't conflict with each other
- The scenario describes wanting to show "base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions" but different jurisdictions have different legal classifications and requirements


- The real problem is that the feature spec is uniform across all regions, but the legal and regulatory landscape isn't. I need to figure out what "deductions" means for employees versus contractors, what "real time" actually entails, and whether Germany's works council approval is already in place or still pending. The core ambiguity is whether we're building one earnings display that works everywhere or customizing it per jurisdiction. I'm settling on `ambiguity` as the classification since the core requirement—showing an earnings breakdown—gets interpreted differently depending on jurisdiction-specific labor laws, and the spec doesn't clarify which version applies. My confidence stays around 55% because of this inherent vagueness in how the feature should actually work across different legal contexts.
